topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crumpled manuscript lay on the coffee table, a stark white expanse mocking me. I picked up the nearest page, my fingers tracing the elegant, almost painfully intimate description of my morning routine—the way I habitually poured my tea, the slight furrow in my brow when I read the news. A slow, heavy ache settled in my chest. I reread a passage about my peculiar habit of humming off-key while cooking, and a tight knot tightened in my stomach. "Are you serious?" I muttered, the sound barely escaping my throat. I pushed the book away with unnecessary force, the sound of the paper scraping against the wood echoing the sharp edge of my irritation. I needed a moment, somewhere quiet, before I could even begin to process the sheer audacity of this project.

[story 2]

I found the laptop open on the bedside table, the screen glowing with a half-finished chapter titled, "The Architect of Quiet Moments." My breath hitched. Every sentence detailed my insecurities, the way I retreat when things get too intense, the almost invisible tremor in my hand when I’m nervous. I stared at the words, my jaw clenching so hard my teeth ached. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small room, my movements jerky and restless. "This is ridiculous," I whispered to the empty air. I walked over to the desk, my shadow falling across the glowing screen, and deliberately slammed my hand down on the keyboard, the sudden noise jarring the silence. I needed a complete reset.

[story 3]

It was the third time this week I’d stumbled upon evidence: a grocery list annotated with character notes, a stray bookmark tucked into a well-worn paperback that detailed my favorite obscure film. Now, the evidence was staring me down in the form of a full-length narrative centered entirely on my existence. I leaned against the doorframe, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, trying to keep my breathing even. The sheer weight of it—this intense, unsolicited portrayal of my life—was pressing down on my shoulders. "Stop," I managed, my voice dangerously low and steady, though the tremor in my hands betrayed the internal turmoil. I took a deep, shuddering breath, forcing myself to look away from the screen, needing distance from the overwhelming reality unfolding before me.
